sentences.text: Yes.
Plaszow was a, was a big camp.
They brought people from all over-- from Rabka, from Zakopane, from Nowy Targ, from all over.
From all over.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Inside the-- after we build the camp, inside the camp we build another camp, small camp inside the large camp.
Was a Polish camp.
They used to bring Polish people from outside [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] because, you know, they were behind the fences.
They didn't do work like other-- just kept them down there.
I wasn't that long after we built the camp for them.
But I went to a new camp in the Plaszow.
They used to bring people from outside.
Remember I told you about my aunt, my older, my mother's older sister, Regina, and the two daughters, Anda and Frydka?
One was the professor of the university, the Jagiellonian University in Krakow, and the other one was a dressmaker.
When the Final Solution came in Nowy Targ August the 30, they were prepared for that.
They made themself Aryan papers, as a Aryan, and they escape.
Nobody knew where they went because nobody-- everybody went-- I went to my, to the camp, and my mom and my sister went to the gas chambers as far as that.
But they were living in Krakow.
I didn't even know.
I didn't know what happened to them.
I thought they went with my mother, everything, everybody else.
So one time they brought in people from outside, from-- they were captured on Aryan papers, Jewish people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was assigned with a few other people to buried these people.
In our camp in Plaszow, we have two hills.
They're called Chujowa Gorka, was a ditch.
I don't want to say in English what it mean.
And Hujowa [INAUDIBLE].
So used to kill the people, used to lay them down there, cover them up with stones, every day, every day until the hill was filled up, level.
Then they start on the other hill, Hujowa Gorka.
So one time I was assigned to bury people, me and few friends of mine.
So I buried the people, and all of a sudden-- this was the worst thing happen to me.
It changed my life completely after this.
I see my aunt and my cousin.
They were killed.
They brought them in from outside.
Somebody recognized them, and they were captured.
And they brought them, and they killed them.
I couldn't, I couldn't come to myself after this.
I was so depressed.
I didn't care what happened, you know?
This was the worst thing because I buried other people who would-- didn't mean a thing, because people were killed every day, but it's different when you have to bury your own.
The way they went-- I never forget that.
I don't want to even talk about it for years.
You know, it was, was very hard on me to see my aunt-- you know, the way she was killed-- and my cousins.
But you know, as long as you were in the camp, you got harder, harder.
You got stronger, stronger.
You had a will to live.
I always want to live.
[SIGHS] In the camp-- life was very hard in Plaszow because you have to put up not only so much with the outside, the Ukrainians, also with the Jewish police, [INAUDIBLE].
Sometimes they were worse than the rest of them.
I had a cousin.
She was from Plaszow.
They brought her from the ghetto [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because the women were separate, they slept in different barracks than the men.
But some people, they were married.
They used to get together with each other and so forth on, you know?
Sometime the women came, and they slept overnight, too, with their husbands.
Sometimes they stay with their boyfriends, too, you know?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Myself, I didn't had so bad in, in Plaszow, really, because after work, I got myself a job in the kitchen.
A fellow I used-- I knew-- me and my friend Benek Kalfus, he lives in New Jersey, we knew them.
We-- give us a job.
So after work, we used to go to the kitchen, clean out the kettles, used to cook the soup for the people in camps.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
As a matter of fact, you know, this was a, a-- this was, like, they called it kosher kitchen, this particular.
It was different kind of food than the rest of them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's funny thing.
I could never figure out why do they keep like a kosher food?
It wasn't kosher food, I'm sure, but it was a different kind of food for the, for some people.
So I had a job, so I always had something to eat.
So I don't have so bad in Plaszow.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One time we received a shipment, barracks came in.
Was a one Sturmfuhrer, his name was [NON-ENGLISH], in Plaszow.
He was a murderer.
He was no good.
We had to go to the railroad station, empty the barracks, carry it on my shoulders, you know, parts because all were prefab, to our camps.
And this guy used to run around with his pistol, used to, used to kill people like nothing.
This was a terrible, terrible night.
I never seen so many people were killed like this particular night.
This was in evening.
I don't know why.
Nobody was escaping.
He maybe would just-- he was killing.
He was, he was a, he was a German, name was [NON-ENGLISH].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One time, the next day, when we put up in the barracks, we were putting up the barracks, and I had made a trade with one of the [NON-ENGLISH] for bread.
I give him gold teeth.
And I was caught trading with him by one of the-- Schmidt.
Was a-- he was one of the commander in our camp, too.
His name was Schmidt.
And I was caught.
So meantime, he scolded this Ukrainian, and he tooks a pistol up and make me go on my knees.
He was going to shoot me, kill me.
I start to cry.
I hug onto his boots.
I kissed his feet, and I cry.
I cry.
I beg him.
I wouldn't do it.
I wouldn't do this again.
Finally, he said to me, I'm going to let you go this time and then hit me with the pistol on the head.
You go to work, but I catch you again, I'll kill you right there on the spot.
I was so lucky.

From then on, you know, I was afraid to do anything because I was-- you know, I used to-- I wasn't that really hungry because I had food to eat from the kitchen I used to work, but I used to trade in for other things with some other friends of mine.
We used to do business, you know, with the bread.
What else can you do with the gold, with the gold teeth?
For me it was-- for us we used to work, clean up the graves.
Wasn't so bad because I used to get the gold teeth from-- we used to pull out.
Nobody cared as far as that.
So I spent down there in Plaszow till [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about nine months, I believe.
Something like that.
I was-- we were round up and send out to different camp in Ostrowiec.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was my third camp.
Was a very small camp, too.
I used to work in a steel factory.
We were still wear our own clothes, and they still call you by the name.
There was no numbers then yet.
Nothing.
Still wear civilian clothes and so forth on.
Was a-- huh?
There was still people from Nowy Targ, yes.
I went with my group because my friends from Nowy Targ, they were, they were transport, and I want to stick with them.
I escape, but I went back because I didn't want to leave my friends.
I thought to myself, whatever happen to them going to happen to me.
No.
From the group.
From the group.
I didn't want to go on the transport, because some friends of mine from my hometown remained Plaszow, in Plaszow to the last, till they liquidated camp.
But my best friends, they were going to different camps, so I thought to myself, I'm going to go with them because they're my best friends.
We stick together all this time.
So what is going to happen to them, going happen to me.
So I went with them.
We went to Ostrowiec, small camp.
Was a very short time down there.
I worked in, in the steel factory.
We used to work in the big ovens.
Used to load up the wagons, the steel wagon, with the steel, all kind of junk, and I push them to the metal shop.
I used to make the steels to melt in big ovens.
Was hard work, especially when, when transport came with a row of steel from Norway.
Used to bring the stone.
It looks like a stone, but it's very heavy.
Use-- don't use no shovel.
Use like a fork.
And at the end of the fork is like a ball so those inject into the floor on the train and the car.
[INAUDIBLE].
Was very, very hard work.
My hands was like a, like a concrete, so hard.
I could have drove a nail with my hand.
Took me years, I say years, good couple years to scrape off the, the skin from my hands after Liberation.
So I worked in there till [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1943 when the Russian invading.
They were coming, I believe, in Ostrowiec, something like that.
In my mind was '43, yeah.
End of '43, yes.
Was in '44 maybe, yeah.
Finally, the Russian, they were invading.
They were coming over.
So they liquidated camp.
And they transport us in the boxcars to Auschwitz.
We came to Birkenau.
We arrive always at night.
Usually a transport they used to arrive now.
[INAUDIBLE] they'd keep you overnight in the train.
We stay all night in the train.
They kept us all night, and our transport especially.
They keep us all night in the train.
Early in the morning we heard the dogs barking.
You could see through the window we were-- I didn't know whether we were there.
We didn't know about Auschwitz, really.
We heard a dog bark and open up the door, [NON-ENGLISH], the SS troopers, and in with the dogs.
And I see people with, like, in pajamas running around, blue and white stripes, uniforms.
They told us this is a labor camp.
So we came to-- on the platform.
This was-- must've been a few thousand of us from this camp, from Ostrowiec.
And they were, they were sort us in different groups, the younger people, men separate, women separate, the older people separate, children.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So we were standing in line down there.
And I didn't know about Dr.
Mengele, nothing like that.
I have to interrupt you just to ask you-- at Ostrowiec, there were women also at the camp?
Yes.
There were women also in the camp because they were brought-- the camp was built from the Jews from the ghetto.
There were families-- women, men, children, even children.
Little children, too.
Yeah.
But we came over from Krakow-Plaszow.
We had a separate barracks down in Ostrowiec.
They didn't want nothing to do with us.
They used to live there a good life down in the camp.
They used to live in a family, with their families.
Husband and wives and the families in the barrack, but they were in a camp.
But we had a separate camp.
We already-- I already two camps behind me.
This is my third camp.
They didn't know about camps yet.
So we worked down there, there was-- they didn't like us too well.
They used to call us all kind of names.
No.
I didn't know.
Nothing.
No.
No.
No, the commandant was so close-guarded we didn't know what was going on in other camps till I came to Auschwitz.
Auschwitz was a large camp.
It opened up-- when they took us in the groups, we had to stay sorted, different groups.
And the Dr.
Mengele was standing with his hands like that.
I don't know.
Later on find out he was Dr.
Mengele.
I didn't know who he was.
We all had to go through him.
You know, I was just a little fellow.
You know, he spread my chest and he stand look at me over, used to go [INAUDIBLE].
Went to the right.
Left, right, left, right, left.
So he said to me, looked me over, 'links.' So I go to the 'links.' Supposed to go the 'links,' I went to the right.
I was so upset.
This was the first really huge camp.
You couldn't, you couldn't see from one end to another.
And you see these, the chimneys.
There was smoke.
It was at Birkenau.
You could smell the smoke come.
I didn't know what it was.
Thought it was some kind of factory.
Instead of go to the left, I went to right.
So one SS start to chase me out with the whip.
You're supposed to go to the right.
Saved my life, because the other people went to the right went to the gas chamber right away with older people and the women, children.
So we went-- they took us.
They shave us outside.
There's no room for us when we arrive to [NON-ENGLISH].
By the crematorium was a big [NON-ENGLISH], like a-- you go take a shower, you know?
They shave you.
They spray you with the powder.
They gave you uniform.
We go to [NON-ENGLISH], and right next door was another barrack like that.
That was a big building.
This was the crematorium.
People used to go in down there, and there was nobody come out.
So we took a shower, bunch of us.
We get out.
We were naked, running around naked in the camp.
Then they gave us a uniform.
Everybody had to go get the uniforms.
They just give you a pair of pants, jacket, and shirt and pair of wooden shoes.
There was no socks, no underwear, no such a thing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So there-- your uniform fit or not.
If it was too small or too big, used to try to trade with somebody else.
They give you-- so they give you bowl and a spoon.
You couldn't do without it, I remember that.
This was the most important thing.
You didn't have no bowl, you starved to death.
You didn't have no spoon, you drink without a spoon.
Spoon was a very important because this was your weapon.
This was your spoon to eat.
This was your knife.
This was everything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So I got my uniforms and wooden shoes.
Your wooden shoes-- everybody wore the same size.
There was no sizes, just wooden shoes.
And my pants were a little bit too big for me.
They were short.
And they were too big for me.
So I traded with another guy.
So it wasn't so bad.
So we stayed outside, like there's no room for us.
There's nowhere to put us.
So right next door was a gypsy camp with gypsies.
This was in Birkenau.
So what they did, they took the gypsies out, put them in a gas chamber.
They burned them all.
They made room for us.
How did you know that they were gypsies?
Well, but they had a separate camp.
Was fenced in.
Oh, you could tell.
You could what they look like because you live with them before the war, you know?
And they speak different-- they were between themselves, they speak different languages, you know.
We knew they were gypsies.
We seen them.
They were taking them out to the other barrack.
Nobody came out.
Killed them all.
They made room for us.
Next day, it was time to give us the numbers.
Some guys, they got the numbers.
I didn't get my numbers right away.
Right here, the numbers.
I didn't get my number right away, because I work in Birkenau for a few months.
Then when I was transferred to IG Farben, to Buna, I got my number.
So I got them.
We stay in a line for the numbers.
And one SS trooper was walking around with-- they used to walk around with the cane, and some carried the whips, leather whips.
And one SS man passed me by.
He bumped me.
He said to me, hey, you step on my toe.
I say, I did not.
Made me go on my knees and gave me 25 lashes on my back with the cane.
From then on, I was a problem with the-- with my back.
I pay the price now.
I couldn't even scream.
I bit my tongue.
I couldn't be-- because if I would complain, I would cry, you know?
I knew-- I learned very fast.
You learn very quick.
The ration was terrible.
So I worked in Birkenau.
Used to build the roads.
There were bunch of womens.
They were building the road, too.
The women used to wear a uniform like us, too, but instead of pants, they wear skirts.
They used to wear the, like, a babushka around their head because they shaved their heads.
They need their hair.
They were using their hair for the material.
When you make suits for the uniform, you line them.
That's what they were using their hair for.
They shave us, too.
And this first time I got a number.
And my name was no more Romek Reibeisen.
From then on my name was B5188.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was my name.
So then we got numbers here on our uniform also with a triangle because-- I didn't know what the triangle was for, but you learn quickly from other fellows.
So my triangle was red with the yellow bar above, which they knew who I was, the SS troopers.
They knew red represent Polish and yellow bar a Jew.
So they knew I was a Polish Jew.
The German Jews had a different, different color again, the bar.
There were homosexual.
There were murderers.
There were all kind of people.
There weren't only Jewish people in Auschwitz, in concentration camps.
Auschwitz was named after the camp, was named after the city of Auschwitz.
There were three different camps.
So I work in Birkenau.
My job was, was to build the roads there.
Then we used to clean up the canal.
In between the electric fences, there's a canal with water.
If you tried to escape, you had to go over the water and again the-- nobody actually could escape.
Maybe some were breaking, I don't know.
As far as I know-- but I could never-- I seen many people that just commit suicide.
They just run to the electric fences.
They didn't want to live any longer.
So we used to clean up the canals, always, always to find people laying dead down there.
They used to kill themself.
It was a lot of stuff was growing, algae, stuff like that.
Smelled terrible from the water because it wasn't running water.
The water just lays in the, in the trenches like.
From then on, I was sent to, to Buna, Monowitz.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We marched to the town from Birkenau to Buna, marched about three or five miles.
We marched like that.
There were few-- bunch of us.
We came to Buna.
There's no place for us again, so they put up tents.
There were two big tents.
So we slept in the tents for a while till I find final quarters in the barracks, which I slept on the barrack number 28.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was assigned to build, to baukommando, the building kommando, because they knew what I was doing.
They had my record.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I was again in a building kommando.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And every-- there were-- we were divided on the groups, for instance, when you go to work.
My kommando number was 48.
There were 80 of us, maybe, something like that.
And every group had his leader which we called kapo.
My kapo was a Jewish fellow.
He was a German fellow.
He was a great guy.
His name was [NON-ENGLISH].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
He was a great fellow.
He never harm us.
Sometime the kapo cooperated with the SS troopers, you know?
They used to beat the hell out of you, too, the kapos.
Every morning we used to get up around 5 o'clock in the morning from our barracks.
Had to go to the latrine to wash up, go to the bathroom, whatever it be.
In the latrine were the big speakers.
All around were a bunch of maybe around 50 speakers, big, huge speakers.
And the water was coming, cold water, naturally.
There was no soap.
Just wash your face.
You had a pair of pants-- no underpants-- jacket, and a shirt and go to the bathroom.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was no sit down-- was a bathroom was a piece of board.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [LAUGHS] It was a big hole.
Then you-- whatever had to be done, you did it.
There was no paper to wipe yourself, either.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When you're a slave, you're a slave, I guess.
Then back to the barracks. 6 o'clock in the morning, we were getting our ration-- a slice of bread, like a toast bread.
Black bread made out of sawdust and cornmeal.
And a coffee, black coffee, if you want to call it coffee.
Many times, sometime when things were good for the German, like for Christmastime or something, like some kind of holidays, used to get a piece of margarine, a piece of jello, something like that.
For Christmas I remember I used to get a cigarette.
You had choice to get two cigarette or sniff tobacco.
I used to get the-- I like sniff tobacco because last longer.
I used to take a piece of cement paper, spit them on them and sprinkle and roll them and smoke them.
That's what I like better.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We used to get-- then when we used to get up, got our breakfast, then we're to go to the appellplatz.
We were divided into groups.
Everybody knew where to go already.
You had your own particular place with the groups then.
We stay like that from about 6 o'clock in the morning till around 7:30, 8 o'clock, rain or shine, stay like that like a soldier.
An SS trooper used to walk around.
Then we start to go to work.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At the gates, orchestra play marches.
We used to march to work.
The kapo, the, the commander of our camp, stay outside every day.
And the kapo had to stand, took off his, his hat and report to the commander that his group number is 48, so many, so many men.
We marched 10 in a row.
Count us out.
We march to work.
I can't think.
They hang him after the war.
I know that.
I can't think of it.
My mind, my memory's forgetful already.
Yeah.
